data.title: FreshRealm Files for Bankruptcy After Recalls, Walmart Loss — Update 2
data.description:
By Josh Beckerman

FreshRealm, which provides meal kits for Blue Apron and Marley Spoon, filed for bankruptcy, citing disruptions due to listeria-related recalls and withdrawals that resulted from contaminated ingredients from suppliers.

Blue Apron sales account for about 70% of total revenue, according to the declaration of Chief Financial Officer Bryan Fleming.

The company voluntarily filed for relief in the U.S. Bankruptcy Court for the District of New Jersey on Monday.

FreshRealm said in a statement after the filing it expects to continue normal daily operations and said it reached an agreement with Blue Apron to resolve certain issues. Subject to court approval, Blue Apron would exit its FreshRealm commercial agreement and transition the related operations to a new structure. Blue Apron's exclusive fulfillment business will transition to Misfits Market Inc., Fleming said.

FreshRealm will pursue a sale of its remaining business and operations.

In 2023, FreshRealm bought Blue Apron's operational infrastructure assets in a transaction valued at up to $50 million and acquired some Marley Spoon assets in 2024.

In September, the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention said epidemiologic, laboratory, and traceback data showed that certain meals produced by FreshRealm were contaminated with listeria, and said that pasta from Nate's Fine Foods was an ingredient in FreshRealm meatball marinara linguine meals.

Fleming said that while FreshRealm was implementing a turnaround plan that included reducing facility‑level fixed costs, Walmart told the company that it would cease its customer relationship in January. Walmart had comprised more than 20% of the company's revenue.

Fleming said recalls and withdrawals disrupted operations and hurt customer demand and commercial opportunities. FreshRealm estimates total business interruption losses of about $27.9 million attributable to the 2024-2025 insurance policy period.

In April 2025, a presumptive positive environmental swab was identified on a conveyor belt at the Montezuma, Ga., facility. In May 2025, Walmart withdrew all products produced at that facility as a precautionary measure, Fleming said. FreshRealm received contaminated ingredients in 2025 in linguine, cauliflower and spinach products.

Marley Spoon said Tuesday that "no service interruptions are to be expected for the time being," but said there may be a significant adverse impact on revenue, particularly for U.S. operations.

Blue Apron is owned by Wonder.

Write to Josh Beckerman at josh.beckerman@wsj.com

data.title: Airbus Reports Lower Q1 2026 Revenue And Profit
data.description:
SCHIPHOL-RIJK (dpa-AFX) - Airbus SE (EADSY), an aerospace and defense manufacturing company, reported its first-quarter 2026 financial results on Tuesday.

Revenue decreased to 12.65 billion euros in the first quarter of 2026, down from 13.54 billion euros in the first quarter of 2025. Net income declined to 586 million euros from 793 million euros in the prior-year quarter. Earnings per share fell to 0.74 euros from 1.01 euros in the same period last year.

The company's profit for the period totaled 551 million euros, compared with 745 million euros in the prior-year period. EBIT dropped to 224 million euros from 473 million euros year-over-year.

EADSY is currently trading at $47.43, down $1.20 or 2.47 percent on the OTC Markets.

data.title: Precious Metals Settle Lower Ahead of Fed Decision — Market Talk
data.description: Gold and silver futures finish lower for the second consecutive session, ahead of tomorrow's decision from the Federal Reserve. Markets generally expect interest rates to be unchanged, but the real question is what kind of guidance the Fed will impart regarding the economic fallout from the ongoing conflict in the Middle East. Further downside is expected for gold, says Michael Boutros of Forex.com in a note. "Gold is testing a major support zone between $4,492 and $4,540 [per troy ounce] defined by historical closes and Fibonacci retracement levels," says Boutros. "A confirmed break below support opens downside potential toward the $4,400 level and deeper corrective territory." Front-month gold closes down 1.8% to $4,591.50/oz, while silver falls 2.4% to $73.205/oz. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

All prices as of 1815 GMT

EQUITIES

GLOBAL - Stocks fell and oil prices rose on Tuesday as investors assessed the stalemate in the Iran conflict and news the United Arab Emirates was cutting ties with OPEC, while concerns the AI boom was losing momentum weighed on equity markets.

MSCI's gauge of stocks across the globe EURONEXT:IACWI fell 8.04 points, or 0.75%, to 1,066.96.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

NEW YORK - The Nasdaq Composite fell on Tuesday, underperforming the benchmark S&P 500 and the blue-chip Dow, after a report that said OpenAI had missed its internal revenue target raised fresh concerns about the AI spending spree.

At 11:49 a.m. ET, the Nasdaq Composite dropped 357.34 points, or 1.44%, to 24,529.76. The S&P 500 slipped 55.80 points, or 0.78%, to 7,118.11, while the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 60.16 points, or 0.12%, to 49,227.01.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

LONDON - European shares ended near a three-week low on Tuesday as mixed corporate earnings, uncertainty over the Middle East conflict, and caution ahead of major central bank decisions later this week weighed on sentiment.

The pan-European STOXX 600 declined 0.4% to 606.58 points.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

TOKYO - Japan's Nikkei share average slipped from a record high, government bonds swung and the yen rallied on Tuesday, after the central bank struck a hawkish tone as it kept interest rates unchanged.

The benchmark Nikkei 225 Index fell 1% to close at 59,917.46.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

SHANGHAI - China and Hong Kong stocks fell on Tuesday as the artificial intelligence-led rally took a breather, leaving investors scouring for fresh catalysts to sustain the upward momentum.

By the close, China's blue-chip CSI300 Index was down 0.3%, while the Shanghai Composite Index traded 0.2% lower.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

AUSTRALIA - Australian shares closed at their lowest in nearly four weeks on Tuesday as stalled U.S.-Iran peace talks and caution ahead of domestic inflation data kept investors on edge.

The S&P/ASX 200 index closed 0.6% lower at 8,710.70 points, its lowest finishing level since April 2.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

SEOUL - South Korean shares hit a record high on Tuesday, led by gains in automakers and steel manufacturers, with investor focus on major corporate earnings.

The benchmark KOSPI rose 25.99 points, or 0.39%, to settle at a closing record high of 6,641.02.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

FOREIGN EXCHANGE

NEW YORK - The dollar strengthened on Tuesday as risk-off sentiment driven by the Iran war outweighed a brief yen rally sparked by the Bank of Japan’s most divided policy vote under Governor Kazuo Ueda.

The U.S. dollar index snapped a two-day losing streak to trade 0.2% higher at 98.66.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

SHANGHAI - China's yuan traded in a narrow range against the dollar on Tuesday, as traders focused on this week's upcoming meeting of the U.S. Federal Reserve for clues to the path of interest rate policy.

By 0300 GMT, the spot yuan was 0.04% lower at 6.8305 against the dollar, after trading in a range of 6.8270 to 6.8313.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

AUSTRALIA - The Australian dollar retreated from a 36-year top on the yen on Tuesday as the Bank of Japan held rates but saw more dissent, while investors awaited a domestic inflation report that could seal the case for more policy tightening.

The Aussie slipped 0.3% to 114.22 yen , having hit a 36-year peak of 114.7 yen earlier in the day as traders bet on widening interest rate differentials before the BOJ decision.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

SEOUL - The South Korean won was steady against the U.S. dollar.

The won was quoted at 1,473.6 per dollar on the onshore settlement platform , unchanged from its previous close.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

TREASURIES

NEW YORK - U.S. Treasury yields rose in tandem with higher oil prices on Tuesday with the 10-year yield reaching a three-week high, as mounting doubts over a near-term resolution to the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran stoked concerns about prolonged energy disruptions.

The yield on benchmark U.S. 10-year notes rose 2.3 basis points to 4.36%.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

LONDON - Euro zone bond yields hit multi-week highs on Tuesday after a survey showed euro zone consumers expecting higher inflation, prompting markets to raise the likelihood of a hike in interest rates by the European Central Bank in the coming months.

The euro zone benchmark, Germany's 10-year yield reached 3.086%, again a two-week high. It was last 3.3 bps higher at 3.0718%. (DE10YT=RR)

For a full report, click on

- - - -

TOKYO - Yields on benchmark Japanese government bonds edged towards a 29-year high on Tuesday as investors awaited a policy decision from the central bank and signals for future rate hikes.

The benchmark 10-year JGB yield (JP10YTN=JBTC) rose 0.5 basis point (bp) to 2.475% in early trading, closing in on the 2.49% level hit earlier this month that was the highest since June 1997.

For a full report, click on

COMMODITIES

GOLD

Gold fell to a near four-week low on Tuesday, on peristent inflation concerns after U.S. President Donald Trump appeared dissatisfied with Iran's latest proposal to end the war, while investors awaited the Federal Reserve's monetary policy meeting this week.

Spot gold dropped 1.7% to $4,600.61 per ounce, as of 1:42 p.m. EDT (1742 GMT), after hitting its lowest level since April 2 earlier.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

IRON ORE

Iron ore futures fell on Tuesday after two consecutive sessions of gains, as supply concerns eased following news that China's state buyer had allowed some local steelmakers to purchase previously banned BHP cargoes piling up at ports.

The most-traded September iron ore contract on China's Dalian Commodity Exchange (DCE) fell 0.89% to 780.5 yuan ($114.20) a metric ton.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

BASE METALS

Copper prices fell to their two-week low on Tuesday due to a stronger dollar, concerns about global economic growth and fears over inflation fuelled by lingering uncertainty over the Middle East conflict.

Benchmark three-month copper on the London Metal Exchange was 1.3% lower at $13,042 a metric ton by 1600 GMT after hitting $12,937.5, for its lowest since April 13.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

OIL

Oil prices pared earlier gains after the United Arab Emiratessaid it would leave OPEC and OPEC+, easing some supply concerns, though prices held near the upper end of a nearly 3% rally as stalled efforts to end the Iran war kept the Strait of Hormuz largely closed and constrained Middle East supplies.

Brent crude futures for June climbed $2.93, or 2.7%, to $111.16 a barrel at 11:52 a.m. ET or 1532 GMT.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

PALM OIL

Malaysian palm oil futures were little changed on Tuesday, as weakness in Dalian palm olein offset stronger crude oil prices.

The benchmark palm oil contract for July delivery on the Bursa Malaysia Derivatives Exchange added 3 ringgit, or a marginal 0.07%, to close at 4,537 ringgit ($1,148.90) a metric ton.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

RUBBER

Japanese rubber futures rose for a fifth consecutive session on Tuesday, as tight supply kept physical prices firm, while higher oil prices added to bullish sentiment.

The Osaka Exchange (OSE) rubber contract for October delivery , was up 6.5 yen, or 1.62%, at 407 yen ($2.55) per kg.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

data.title: Univest Financial 10-Q: $87.5M Revenue, $0.96 Diluted EPS
data.description:
Univest Financial reported consolidated revenue of $87.5 million for the quarter, driven by $63.4 million of net interest income and $24.1 million of noninterest income, and delivered diluted earnings per share of $0.96 (basic $0.97). The company recorded operating income (income before income taxes) of $33.5 million and net income of $27.092 million, an increase of $4.697 million (21.0%) versus the prior-year quarter. Results reflect higher tax-equivalent net interest income and fee income partially offset by modest margin compression from liquidity management and higher noninterest expenses.

Financial Highlights

  • Total revenue: $87.5 million (consolidated; Net interest income $63.4M + Noninterest income $24.1M)
  • Operating income (Income before income taxes): $33.5 million
  • Net income: $27.092 million (up $4.697M, or 21.0% year-over-year)
  • Diluted earnings per share: $0.96 (basic $0.97; diluted up from $0.77 in prior-year quarter)
  • Noninterest expense: $52.669 million (includes a $0.427 million restructuring charge)

Business Highlights

  • Revenue drivers: Growth was driven by an 11.7% increase in tax-equivalent net interest income and higher fee income from wealth and other channels.
  • Wealth management: Assets under management rose to $5.8 billion, supporting higher advisory fees.
  • Mortgage and insurance: Mortgage banking salable volume increased and commercial insurance premiums grew, contributing to noninterest income expansion.
  • Operational efficiency and restructuring: Noninterest expense increased 6.8% due to higher salaries and benefits and a $0.4 million restructuring charge related to two branch closures.
  • Liquidity and funding: Management of excess liquidity modestly reduced net interest margin; seasonal deposit declines led to continued reliance on core deposits and available wholesale funding capacity.

Original SEC Filing: UNIVEST FINANCIAL Corp [ UVSP ] - 10-Q - Apr. 28, 2026

Disclaimer
This is an AI-powered summary. It may contain inaccuracies. Consider verifying important information with the source. Please note this summary is solely based on documents filed with the SEC.

data.title: California's Wealth Tax Gains Steam. Billionaires Fight Back. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Abby Schultz

A tax on California's billionaires took a step forward this week as proponents of the one-time tax said they collected enough signatures to put the measure on the November ballot.

The California Billionaire Tax Coalition announced Monday that they were filing more than 1.55 million signatures with the state attorney general and county registrars, more than the 874,641 required to get an initiative before voters. Before the proposal — which would levy a 5% tax on the net worth of billionaires living in the state as of Jan. 1, 2026 — can get on the November ballot the signatures need to be verified by county registrars.

Several high-profile billionaires — among them Google co-founder Sergey Brin, former Google CEO Eric Schmidt, and Ripple Labs founder Chris Larsen — are pushing back hard against the proposal, fighting the tax and offering support for measures that would in some cases nullify the tax's impacts and in others, address pressing issues such as housing, education, and affordability.

California's billionaire tax is the most well-known and toughest effort yet to tax the wealthy on a local or state level. But it is hardly the only one.

In New York City, Mayor Zohran Mamdani has been pushing for measures to "tax the rich" since his campaign last year so that he can raise revenue to address the city's budget shortfall. The mayor recently endorsed a proposal by New York Gov. Kathy Hochul, a Democrat, to levy a tax on the city's pieds-à-terre worth $5 million or more, and drew the ire of Citadel founder and CEO Ken Griffin, one of the city's most prominent billionaires, when he singled out his $238 million penthouse as an example.

The proposal, which Hochul said could raise $500 million annually, is currently being considered as part of budget negotiations with the state legislature.

There are other cases across the country, including Washington, which recently passed 9.9% tax on those who make $1 million or more. In Maryland, legislators rejected a wealth tax, but created new tax brackets for high income earners, including a 6.5% tax on single filers earning more than $1 million and joint filers earning more than $1.2 million. The state also enacted a 2% surtax on net capital gains if a taxpayer's federal adjusted gross income is more than $350,000, unless the gains were from a primary home that sold for less than $1.5 million.

The proposed California tax is expected to generate $100 billion in revenue, helping to plug a gap in federal funding created by cuts included in the federal tax act last summer, according to the Service Employees International Union and United Healthcare Workers West (SEIU-UHW), which is behind the ballot initiative.

Medi-Cal, California's Medicaid program, is expected to lose about $19 billion in federal funding a year, an amount that could total as much as $190 billion over 10 years, proponents of the tax proposal said. About 10% of revenue generated by the billionaire tax would fund K-14 public education and food assistance programs.

Those opposed to the tax — which include California Gov. Gavin Newsom — counter that it will push California's billionaires out of the state and, because of lost revenue from existing taxes, will be costlier to the state in the long run.

"This proposal is poorly constructed, very likely unconstitutional, and economically damaging to the entire state," Brian Brokaw, a veteran political consultant who established Stop the Squeeze, a committee opposed to the tax, with Dan Newman, also a veteran consultant. Brokaw and Newman have ties to Newsom, a Democrat.

On Monday, Stop the Squeeze published an economic impact analysis that found the tax would lead to a cumulative net loss of $49.7 billion between 2027 and 2046 (in constant 2026 dollars).

This is based on estimates the tax would raise slightly more than $50 billion (in constant 2026 dollars) over five years, and would lead to a reduction in personal income tax collected of $177 million next year, $3.1 billion in 2028, expanding to $13.5 billion by 2046 as billionaires worth $1 trillion flee the state.

Those departures and more would also lead to 108,000 lost jobs and $28 billion in lost wages, according to the analysis, conducted by two California-based consulting firms — Capitol Matrix Consulting and Full Moon Strategies.

"Enacting a so-called wealth tax in just one state wouldn't target a small group — it would impact all 40 million Californians," Brokaw said. "That's why opposition is so broad across the political spectrum...This proposal trades a short-term revenue bump for long-term losses, forcing cuts to schools and healthcare while shifting the burden onto everyday taxpayers."

A separate study by Stanford University's Hoover Institution estimates that six of the state's approximately 200 billionaires worth $536 billion have already fled the state, taking with them almost 30% of the wealth proponents expected to tax. That alone means the one-time tax will raise only about $40 billion instead of the $100 billion proponents forecast.

The Hoover Institution researchers also estimate billionaires in the state have paid between $3.3 billion and $5.8 billion a year in state income taxes, and that the value of permanently lost income tax "more than fully offsets the onetime wealth tax revenue."

The research shows that the tax "would dig us into a deeper hole rather than filling it," Roger Salazar, a spokesman for Golden State Promise, a group formed by Larsen to oppose the tax, told Barron's. Golden State Promise has teamed up with the California Business Roundtable, to fight the tax.

"There are real consequences to this that could have a real impact on the economy and workforce," Salazar says. Golden State Promise "wants California to be a beacon of innovation."

The Building a Better California initiative, which Brin has financed with $57 million — according to state disclosures — isn't directly fighting the tax, but it is backing several other measures, half of which support affordability and housing, and the other half supporters say will close loopholes and improve government accountability and transparency.

The latter measures include the "Improving Transparency, Effectiveness, and Efficiency in California Government Act," which would effectively nullify the Billionaire Tax by requiring any new taxes adhere to a state spending limit that became law in 1979. Backers of this proposal will be submitting signatures to qualify it for the November ballot later this week, according to a news release.

Another would require any new laws to adhere to state requirements that about 40% of the state budget goes toward public schools, including community colleges. The Billionaire Tax act would funnel 90% of collected revenue to healthcare. Another proposal that would "protect retirement" would ban retroactive taxes, such as the provision in the Billionaire Tax Act that would tax billionaire wealth in the state as of this past January.

In response to these efforts, Suzanne Jimenez, chief of staff of SEIU-UHW, a healthcare union and lead supporter of the billionaire tax, said in a statement, "A small group of billionaires are spending millions to deny Californians the opportunity to save their local hospitals and ERs — but so far, those few billionaires are failing. The public is crystal clear on the fact that keeping ERs and clinics open is more important than billionaires getting more tax breaks."

Proponents of the tax have also eschewed arguments that it would cause billionaire flight from the state. "The overwhelming majority of billionaires in the state appear to have stayed, and the myth of billionaires fleeing California has collapsed," the Billionaire Tax Coalition said on its website.

If federal funding isn't replaced, "hospitals and emergency rooms will close and patients will die," Zelda Aaron, a social worker at Community Hospital of San Bernardino, said in a coalition statement. "Ambulances will in some cases drive twice as far when rural hospitals close and patients will wait twice as long. Businesses and workers will see their premiums doubled."

Write to Abby Schultz at abby.schultz@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Trial Begins Between Elon Musk and Sam Altman for the Future of OpenAI — WSJ
data.description:
By Angel Au-Yeung

Lawyers representing Elon Musk and Sam Altman began opening statements on Tuesday in a landmark trial that could potentially upend OpenAI's future.

"Without Elon Musk, there would be no OpenAI," said Steven Molo, the lead attorney for Musk, who was seated right behind him. Molo sought to highlight Musk's concern about the impact of artificial intelligence on humanity as well as his integral role in starting OpenAI, noting that he helped with recruiting and provided millions in early funding.

Molo also appeared to address an issue that surfaced in jury selection Monday as some potential jurors acknowledged having a negative bias against Musk due to his political advocacy.

"Whether you like him or dislike him, you can't disagree that he is a legend in the tech world," Molo said. He noted Musk's connection to OpenAI's founding in 2015 and said Musk met with President Obama in that year to share his concerns about the advance of AI. A photo of Musk and Obama meeting in the White House flashed on a screen before jurors.

Musk has accused OpenAI and Altman, its CEO, of manipulating him into thinking he was donating tens of millions of dollars to help launch a nonprofit to develop artificial intelligence for the benefit of humanity, only to turn it into a for-profit venture.

OpenAI has said that Musk not only knew about the plan to create a for-profit structure, but that he supported it and requested unilateral control of the venture. The OpenAI founders said no to his request, leading Musk to start his own AI company and now sue as a "broader strategy of harassment aimed at slowing us down," the company has alleged.

The remedies that Musk is asking for include the removal of Altman and OpenAI President Greg Brockman from their leadership roles at the AI company, damages worth more than $180 billion to be paid from OpenAI's for-profit arm to its nonprofit parent, and unwinding the company's recent conversion to a more traditional governance structure.

Legal experts have said Musk is an underdog in the case. Musk and Altman both appeared in court on Tuesday.

On Monday, nine jurors were selected out of several dozen after facing questions from Judge Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers and lawyers from both sides about their potential biases.

Although Musk didn't appear Monday, the trial was clearly on his mind. He posted more than 20 times Monday on X about OpenAI and Altman, including some reposts. As jury selection kicked off, Musk wrote: "Scam Altman and Greg Stockman stole a charity. Full stop."

For the potential jurors who expressed a negative bias toward Musk on written questionnaire forms, the judge pressed them to explain the source of their bias. Nearly all cited Musk's political advocacy.

Judge Gonzalez Rogers told jurors the trial isn't about Musk's political work, but about two parties "who disagree about what happened factually." She then asked those potential jurors if they could put aside their biases toward Musk for this trial. Nearly all said they could.

A few potential jurors who expressed distrust toward the AI industry as a whole were also asked about their biases.

After the potential jurors left the courtroom for a recess, Steven Molo, lead lawyer for Musk, tried to get a number of jurors thrown out of consideration, including two who called the billionaire "a piece of garbage" and "a world-class jerk" in their questionnaire forms.

"The reality is, people don't like him," the judge said in response. "Many people don't like him. But that doesn't mean that Americans cannot, nevertheless, have integrity for the judicial process."

Following jury-selection procedure, both sides were allowed to remove a limited number of potential jurors before the court agreed on the final nine.

The trial is taking place in federal court in Oakland, Calif. The presiding judge has handled high-profile tech-industry cases including the long-running antitrust lawsuit between Epic Games and Apple.

Hundreds of legal documents unsealed in recent months as part of the OpenAI lawsuit have offered a rare glimpse into the inner workings of the Silicon Valley elite. The documents range from emails between Musk and Altman to the private diary entries of Brockman.

"We've been thinking that maybe we should just flip to a for profit," Brockman wrote in a private journal entry in 2017, obtained as part of legal discovery. "Making the money for us sounds great and all."

Musk's lawyers pointed to those private journal entries as proof the OpenAI founders "secretly had other plans" about the company's corporate structure, according to a court filing.

News Corp, owner of the Journal, has a content-licensing partnership with OpenAI.

Write to Angel Au-Yeung at angel.au-yeung@wsj.com
